one_line_takeaway: By reframing LLM red-teaming as quality-diversity search over a feature grid, Rainbow Teaming automatically produces large, diverse archives of >90%-ASR black-box jailbreaks that both diagnose and, via synthetic SFT data, repair model vulnerabilities.
key_finding: Rainbow Teaming discovers hundreds of effective adversarial prompts per run with >90% attack success rate across all tested safety-aligned models, while maintaining high diversity, and its output can be recycled as SFT data that nearly eliminates the vulnerability without degrading general capability. Concretely: ~92% ASR on both Llama 2-chat 7B and Llama 3-Instruct 8B, ~98% on Mistral 7B and Vicuna 7B v1.5; on JailbreakBench it jailbreaks far more behaviors (8/7 by the JBB classifier; 66 jailbreaks over 41 behaviors by Llama Guard) than either PAIR variant while keeping lower Self-BLEU (0.51 vs 0.74). Prompts transfer at ~50% of original-target ASR on average (up to 66% on GPT-4o). SFT on RT data cuts ASR from 92%/95% to 0.3%/0.7% (GPT-4/Llama Guard) and PAIR-on-JBB ASR from 14% to 0%, with essentially unchanged GSM8K (0.224->0.219) and MMLU (0.412->0.405).
core_contribution: Rainbow Teaming, a black-box automated red-teaming method that casts adversarial-prompt discovery as a quality-diversity (QD) search problem built on MAP-Elites. Rather than optimizing a single jailbreak for one behavior (as in prior black-box methods it cites, PAIR and TAP), it populates a discrete K-dimensional 'archive' grid whose axes are diversity features (e.g., Risk Category x Attack Style), producing a whole collection of prompts that are simultaneously effective AND diverse. The paper's own novel elements are: (1) framing adversarial prompting as QD/open-ended search (claimed as the first application of a quality-diversity framework to LLM attacks); (2) an LLM Mutator that applies one directed mutation per feature toward a pre-sampled target descriptor (avoiding a post-hoc cell classifier and mitigating mutator bias); (3) a preference-based LLM Judge that pairwise-ranks which of two responses is more unsafe rather than assigning an absolute score (avoiding reward-hacking/saturation); and (4) a parent-child BLEU similarity filter to preserve linguistic diversity. The archive doubles as a diagnostic tool and as synthetic SFT data to harden the target.
threat_model: Black-box adversary/red-teamer with query-only access to a target LLM (no weights, gradients, or logits required; the Target need not even be an LLM). The adversary automatically generates natural-language prompts that elicit unsafe/harmful/toxic responses, incorrect answers (QA domain), or insecure-code/cyberattack assistance (cybersecurity domain). Attack success is judged by external safety classifiers (GPT-4 with a specialized prompt; Llama Guard for per-category classification), not by the method's own judge. The defender-side use is the mirror image: the discovered diverse archive is used as synthetic data to safety-fine-tune the same model.
limitations: Reported by the authors: (1) In cybersecurity, the automated CyberSecEval judge marked 100% of prompts malicious while human experts scored lower (0.80-0.94), signaling an over-optimistic evaluator and the need for better cybersecurity-specific evaluation. (2) SFT on adversarial-only data caused a small (0.5%) drop in the helpfulness reward-model score; the authors suggest mixing in helpfulness data but leave it to future work. (3) Transfer is highly uneven and direction-dependent (e.g., only 0.01 ASR from Mistral 7B -> Llama 2-chat 7B), so prompts are not uniformly general. (4) Reviewer-noted: results depend on LLM judges as ground-truth proxies (human-AI agreement 78-81%), so ASR reflects classifier judgments, not audited harm; the full limitations/broader-impact discussion sits in Appendix A, which was outside the 22-page extraction.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (Chao et al. 2024) - 100 harmful behaviors, 1000 generated prompts
Anthropic Harmless dataset - reward-model safety score
Anthropic Helpful dataset - reward-model helpfulness score
CyberSecEval / Purple Llama (Bhatt et al. 2023) - cybersecurity evaluator
MITRE ATT&CK enterprise categories (10 tactics) - cybersecurity archive axis
Llama Guard safety risk taxonomy (Inan et al. 2023) - basis for Risk Category feature
quantitative_results: Safety ASR (GPT-4): ~92% on Llama 2-chat 7B and Llama 3-Instruct 8B; ~98% on Mistral 7B and Vicuna 7B v1.5, over 2000 iterations / 100-prompt archives
JailbreakBench (best of 4): Rainbow Teaming 8/7 (JBB classifier) and 66/41 (Llama Guard) vs PAIR-with-RT-mutator 1/1 and 14/11; original PAIR -/4
Self-BLEU (diversity, lower better): RT 0.51 vs PAIR-with-RT-mutator 0.74 on JailbreakBench

Similarity-filter ablation (tau=0.6, ~24% discarded): filter ON gives ASR 0.92, Self-BLEU 0.42, BERTScore 0.74, ROUGE-L 0.15, compression 3.10; filter OFF gives ASR 0.99 but Self-BLEU 0.79, BERTScore 0.83, ROUGE-L 0.39, compression 6.35
Transfer ASR (Llama Guard, best of 4): ~50% of own-target ASR on average; diagonal own-target 0.90-0.96; up to 0.66 on GPT-4o; as low as 0.01 (Mistral->Llama 2-chat 7B)

Defense via SFT on 1500 RT prompts (300 held-out): ASR 0.92->0.003 (GPT-4), 0.95->0.007 (Llama Guard); PAIR-on-JBB ASR 0.14->0.0; GSM8K 0.224->0.219; MMLU 0.412->0.405; safety RM 0.883->0.897; helpfulness RM 0.518->0.513
Re-applying RT to the SFT-hardened model: final ASR 39% (down from 92%)
Cybersecurity ASR: CyberSecEval 1.00 on all 4 targets; human-expert ASR 0.94 (Llama 2-chat 7B), 0.80 (Llama 2-chat 70B), 0.92 (CodeLlama 7B), 0.80 (CodeLlama 34B)
QA domain: RT mean fitness 0.91 / coverage 0.97 / Self-BLEU 0.50 vs no-stepping-stones baseline 0.79 / 0.90 / 0.60
Inter-evaluator agreement: human-human 83%, GPT-4-human 81%, Llama Guard-human 78%, GPT-4-Llama Guard 79% (100 pairs)
baselines_compared: PAIR (Chao et al. 2023) - two variants: the MiXtral-based one from JailbreakBench and one using RT's own mutator LLM with N=20 parallel streams (2000 prompts)
No Stepping Stones - ablation ignoring past archive solutions, regenerating from risk category then applying attack-style mutation
Same Cell Mutations - ablation using the parent's own descriptor as the candidate descriptor (no cross-category mutation)
QA No-Stepping-Stones baseline generating questions from scratch each turn
Ablations: preference model vs score-based evaluator (Appendix E.4); system-prompt variants (Appendix E.2)
